steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction / cholangitis; nephrolithiasis; malignant biliary obstruction (given prostate CA history and prior radiotherapy)
ensemble.information_gap: The ultrasound and non-contrast CT already performed show extrahepatic biliary duct dilation (CBD 10 mm) and gallstones, but the exact cause of the ductal dilation — choledocholithiasis, stricture, malignant compression, or cholangitis — has not been characterized, and the full extent of intra-abdominal pathology (including the right ureter given nephrolithiasis history and the bowel given prior radiation) has not been assessed with contrast enhancement.
ensemble.expected_finding: A common bile duct stone or biliary stricture explaining the 10 mm CBD dilation; alternatively, periductal inflammation consistent with cholangitis, or a mass/lymphadenopathy compressing the biliary tree; also assessment of the right kidney/ureter for obstructing stone given history of nephrolithiasis and right-sided pain.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: A contrast-enhanced CT adds value over the already-performed non-contrast CT by better characterizing the biliary tree, detecting choledocholithiasis, assessing for cholangitis/perfusion changes, and evaluating other right-sided pathology, though MRCP or ERCP would be more definitive for biliary pathology; the order is reasonable but not the single most targeted next step.
grounding: History: 'US was performed and he was noted to have extrahepatic biliary duct dilation with CBD to 10 mm and gallstones. CT abd pelvis showed the same.'
History: 'sharp, constant and R-sided' pain waking from sleep
History: 'Prostate CA s/p radiotherapy'
History: 'nephrolithiasis hx gallstones CKD stage III'
Labs: 'lactate of 2.3' (borderline elevated, raising concern for early cholangitis/sepsis)
Labs: 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 6.77 K/uL' with 'Neutrophils: 89.1%' (left shift pattern suggesting inflammatory/infectious process)
Physical examination: 'TTP in RUQ, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL' (not elevated, making complete obstruction less likely but not excluded)
ensemble.reasoning: The ultrasound and prior CT have identified CBD dilation to 10 mm with gallstones but have not clarified whether a stone is impacted in the CBD, whether there is cholangitis (suggested by borderline lactate 2.3 and neutrophil predominance of 89.1%), or whether the prostate cancer history contributes a compressive or metastatic biliary lesion. A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is ordered to better delineate the biliary anatomy, assess for periductal inflammation or perfusion abnormalities consistent with cholangitis, evaluate the right ureter given the nephrolithiasis history, and survey for any malignant process given the oncologic background — all of which would directly guide the next management step (ERCP vs. urology vs. oncology consultation).

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction / cholangitis; nephrolithiasis; malignant biliary obstruction (given prostate CA history and prior radiotherapy)
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The ultrasound and non-contrast CT already performed show extrahepatic biliary duct dilation (CBD 10 mm) and gallstones, but the exact cause of the ductal dilation — choledocholithiasis, stricture, malignant compression, or cholangitis — has not been characterized, and the full extent of intra-abdominal pathology (including the right ureter given nephrolithiasis history and the bowel given prior radiation) has not been assessed with contrast enhancement.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: A common bile duct stone or biliary stricture explaining the 10 mm CBD dilation; alternatively, periductal inflammation consistent with cholangitis, or a mass/lymphadenopathy compressing the biliary tree; also assessment of the right kidney/ureter for obstructing stone given history of nephrolithiasis and right-sided pain.
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: A contrast-enhanced CT adds value over the already-performed non-contrast CT by better characterizing the biliary tree, detecting choledocholithiasis, assessing for cholangitis/perfusion changes, and evaluating other right-sided pathology, though MRCP or ERCP would be more definitive for biliary pathology; the order is reasonable but not the single most targeted next step.

A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is ordered to better delineate the biliary anatomy, assess for periductal inflammation or perfusion abnormalities consistent with cholangitis, evaluate the right ureter given the nephrolithiasis history, and survey for any malignant process given the oncologic background — all of which would directly guide the next management step (ERCP vs. urology vs. oncology consultation).

verification.actual_finding: The CT with contrast showed mild intra- and extrahepatic biliary duct dilation with CBD measuring 12 mm and gallstones, but no choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, periductal inflammation, mass, or lymphadenopathy to explain the ductal dilation, and no obstructing renal/ureteral stone or hydronephrosis.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected to find a specific cause of CBD dilation (stone, stricture, mass, or cholangitis) and possibly a ureteral stone, but the contrast CT identified none of these — leaving the etiology of biliary dilation unexplained and the right-sided pain without a urologic cause.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; biliary colic; early cholangitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the gallbladder wall is thickened, whether there is a sonographic Murphy's sign, and whether a stone is visible in the common bile duct — findings that CT with contrast may undercharacterize compared to dedicated biliary ultrasound
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic edema, or a positive sonographic Murphy's sign suggesting acute cholecystitis; alternatively, a visualized CBD stone confirming choledocholithiasis as the cause of biliary duct dilation
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The CT already demonstrated CBD dilation to 12 mm and gallstones without wall thickening or surrounding inflammation, so a dedicated liver/gallbladder ultrasound adds incremental value mainly for sonographic Murphy's sign and better CBD stone visualization, but much of the anatomic information is already available from the contrast CT.
grounding: Prior imaging CT: 'mild intra and extrahepatic biliary duct dilatation with common bile duct measuring 12 mm'
Prior imaging CT: 'The gallbladder contains gallstones without wall thickening or surrounding inflammation. Trace pericholecystic free fluid noted.'
History: 'US was performed and he was noted to have extrahepatic biliary duct dilation with CBD to 10 mm and gallstones'
History: 'sharp, constant and R-sided' pain waking from sleep, RUQ tenderness on exam
Labs: 'lactate of 2.3', ALT 43 (mildly elevated), total bilirubin 0.6 (normal), lipase 12 (normal)
Physical exam: 'TTP in RUQ, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no organomegaly, no rebound or guarding'
PMH: 'hx gallstones', 'nephrolithiasis', 'CKD stage III'
ensemble.reasoning: With a dilated CBD (12 mm on CT, 10 mm on prior US), known gallstones, RUQ pain, mildly elevated ALT, and a borderline lactate, the physician is most concerned about choledocholithiasis or early cholangitis and wants to determine whether the gallbladder itself is acutely inflamed (cholecystitis) — information best obtained by real-time sonographic Murphy's sign and dynamic assessment of the gallbladder wall, which CT cannot provide. The ultrasound is also better suited to detect a CBD stone at the level of the distal duct, which CT may miss, and would guide the decision between ERCP and cholecystectomy.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; biliary colic; early cholangitis
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether the gallbladder wall is thickened, whether there is a sonographic Murphy's sign, and whether a stone is visible in the common bile duct — findings that CT with contrast may undercharacterize compared to dedicated biliary ultrasound
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic edema, or a positive sonographic Murphy's sign suggesting acute cholecystitis; alternatively, a visualized CBD stone confirming choledocholithiasis as the cause of biliary duct dilation
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The CT already demonstrated CBD dilation to 12 mm and gallstones without wall thickening or surrounding inflammation, so a dedicated liver/gallbladder ultrasound adds incremental value mainly for sonographic Murphy's sign and better CBD stone visualization, but much of the anatomic information is already available from the contrast CT.

The ultrasound is also better suited to detect a CBD stone at the level of the distal duct, which CT may miss, and would guide the decision between ERCP and cholecystectomy.

verification.actual_finding: Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis with sludge and stones at the gallbladder neck, a dilated CBD (10 mm), but no gallbladder wall thickening, no pericholecystic free fluid, no sonographic Murphy's sign documented, and no CBD stone visualized.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The expected findings of gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic edema, or a positive sonographic Murphy's sign suggesting acute cholecystitis were absent, and no CBD stone was identified to confirm choledocholithiasis, partially addressing but not confirming either anticipated diagnosis.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction; less likely biliary stricture or periampullary mass
ensemble.information_gap: Whether a stone (or other obstructing lesion) is lodged in the distal common bile duct or ampullary region — the CBD is dilated to 10-12 mm on both US and CT but no stone was visualized in the distal duct on either study, and the pancreatic head/ampullary region was not adequately assessed
ensemble.expected_finding: A filling defect (stone) within the common bile duct, particularly in the distal CBD or at the ampulla, confirming choledocholithiasis; alternatively, a stricture or mass at the ampulla/pancreatic head that would explain the biliary dilation
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Both US and CT demonstrate CBD dilation (10-12 mm) without a visualized distal stone or clear obstructing lesion, and MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for imaging the biliary tree and detecting choledocholithiasis that CT and US missed.
grounding: Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'mild intra and extrahepatic biliary duct dilatation with common bile duct measuring 12 mm'
Prior imaging 2 (US): 'CBD measures 10 mm and is visualized up to the pancreatic head. No stones identified up to this portion'
Prior imaging 2 (US): 'Cholelithiasis and biliary sludge... Stones and sludge are noted within the gallbladder neck'
Prior imaging 2 (US): 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas'
History: 'extrahepatic biliary duct dilation with CBD to 10 mm and gallstones'
Labs: 'Lactate of 2.3' (elevated, suggesting possible early biliary sepsis/obstruction)
Labs: 'ALT: 43.0 IU/L' (mildly elevated), 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL' (normal)
History: 'hx gallstones', 'sharp, constant and R-sided' pain waking from sleep
ensemble.reasoning: The patient has known gallstones, RUQ pain, and biliary ductal dilation on both US and CT, but neither modality visualized a stone in the distal CBD — the distal duct and ampullary region were not adequately assessed (bowel gas obscured the pancreas on US; CT has limited sensitivity for soft-tissue biliary stones). With a mildly elevated lactate of 2.3 and an elevated anion gap of 21, there is concern for early biliary obstruction possibly progressing toward cholangitis, making it critical to determine whether a CBD stone is present before deciding on ERCP versus conservative management. MRCP is the appropriate next step as it provides high-resolution, non-invasive imaging of the entire biliary tree without radiation or contrast risk.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction; less likely biliary stricture or periampullary mass
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether a stone (or other obstructing lesion) is lodged in the distal common bile duct or ampullary region — the CBD is dilated to 10-12 mm on both US and CT but no stone was visualized in the distal duct on either study, and the pancreatic head/ampullary region was not adequately assessed
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: A filling defect (stone) within the common bile duct, particularly in the distal CBD or at the ampulla, confirming choledocholithiasis; alternatively, a stricture or mass at the ampulla/pancreatic head that would explain the biliary dilation
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: Both US and CT demonstrate CBD dilation (10-12 mm) without a visualized distal stone or clear obstructing lesion, and MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for imaging the biliary tree and detecting choledocholithiasis that CT and US missed.

verification.actual_finding: The MRCP showed a dilated CBD (1.2 cm) with abrupt tapering consistent with papillary stenosis, likely from previously passed stones, without a discrete filling defect or mass identified.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The study resolved the information gap by identifying the cause of biliary dilation at the distal CBD/ampullary region — papillary stenosis from prior stone passage — which is an alternative explanation the physician explicitly listed as an expected finding.
